text.tspan: This project is a Unity-based educational programming game where players solve simple level puzzles by
arranging code-block commands to control a character. The aim is to help prospective computer science
students learn basic programming logic and sequencing through a visual interface that reduces syntax
burden and cognitive overload.
The core gameplay loop is: select movement commands, arrange them into a sequence, run the sequence,
observe the character’s movement, and revise the solution until the objective is completed.
The project was developed as a team software engineering artefact, with the goal of producing a working
prototype demonstrating both educational value and collaborative development practice. The original
concept was narrowed into an MVP focused on grid movement, block execution, UI interaction, collectables/
Introduction
LSEPI
Legal, Social, Ethical
and Professional Issues
The main LSEPI concerns were professional conduct, ethical educational claims, data protection,
inclusivity, and legal use of assets. As the game targets beginner computer science learners, we avoided
overstating its learning impact and treated it as a prototype rather than a validated teaching tool.
Professional practice was informed by BCS/ACM-style expectations around responsibility, public benefit,
avoiding harm, due care, and honest communication. This was reflected through version control, sprint
reviews, task allocation, stakeholder communication, meeting records, and transparent reporting of
project limitations.
Ethically, we did not proceed with open-day user testing without approval. This limited validation, but
avoided collecting feedback from users without the correct process in place. The original plan for server-
side metric storage was also reduced to local-only storage without personal data collection, lowering
privacy and security risk.
Inclusivity was considered through readable visual blocks, simple controls, recognisable UI, progressive
difficulty, and reduced syntax burden. However, full accessibility testing remained outside the reduced
project scope.
Group Reflection
Initial planning was appropriate for a 7-person team, but capacity variance was not accounted for enough.
The plan assumed a relatively even level of contribution, but in practice, multiple members did not engage
with the project or communications. The majority of contributions were completed by a small number of
active members, and this created bottlenecks as team members over-estimated their availability.
The technical learning curve of Unity/C# was underestimated, and unfamiliar features required more
implementation time. Because the project manager and product owner had to become heavily involved in
coding and asset management to cover absences, the team's system engineer was unable to spend as much
time managing architecture as planned. This had a negative impact on high-level technical structure, causing
a series of mid-project refactors and duplicated work.
As the schedule slipped and individual stress increased, shadow-tasking was introduced to support with
individual failure to complete tasks. Working meetings were also introduced after a mid-project slump,
allowing for shared understanding and code review, as opposed to our earlier isolated and asynchronous
feature development.
Task mapping and documentation helped the team adapt to these challenges. Breaking the project into task
chunks and weighting tasks according to prioritization models (such as RICE) meant that the team could make
systematic decisions when reducing scope. This ensured cuts were considered on the basis of value, not
made by guesswork. Task priority mapping helped preserve a working prototype and was the team's
strongest group process.
An adaption for future projects would be planning around contribution variance, including accountability
check-ins, and tackling minimum viable functionality earlier in development.
Further improvements include:
- Accounting for technical ramp-up and allowing for integration/testing periods between sprints.
- Extending shadow tasking to identify 'shadow roles' for clearer ownership when individuals are not
contributing.
- Faster escalation through the team's internal grievance process, as it was lengthy, overly forgiving, and
delayed receiving stakeholder feedback.
by CMP2804 Team Software Engineering Group 19
(Daniel N, James W, Borisz K, Shawn M)
Codestepper
CC0 - https://pixabay.com/photos/puzzle-fits-match-is-missing-693868/
Software Engineering Practices
Our development strategy used a lightweight iterative/agile approach across five three-week sprints. A work
breakdown structure and Gantt chart were used to plan sprint tasks, while weekly meetings acted as review
points. GitHub supported version control through issues, feature branches, pull requests, and stable integration
into the main project.
This approach suited an exploratory Unity project because the team needed flexibility while learning the engine,
integrating unfamiliar systems, and adapting to uneven availability. As schedule pressure increased, later
milestones were reduced so that the team could prioritise a polished core prototype over an incomplete broader
product.
The control/data-flow diagram shows how UI, game state, level/player logic, and persistence interact, including
config loading, level selection, block execution, metrics saving, pause/settings, and level completion.
Implementation
Evaluation
The artefact is a Unity/C# educational coding-block prototype where the player programs a
character using a queue of command blocks. When executed, the queue is interpreted into
player movement within a tilemap-based level.
Unity was chosen because it provides strong 2D tooling, tilemaps, prefab workflows, C#
scripting, and accessible development support. The codebase was split into focused scripts
and prefabs so different features could be developed in parallel with clearer ownership and
fewer merge conflicts.
The project provides a stable foundation for further development, achieving 3/5 of our original
milestone goals and implementing the majority of features within those milestones. Minimum viable
product milestones focused on core functionality, but expanding the game's educational value through
different computer science and programming concepts was next on our roadmap.
Verification was completed through manual testing of core game functions; however, validation was
limited to internal peer review because external user testing required ethics approval. This remains an
identified limitation. To this end, user testing and further accessibility feature implementation would be
chief amongst future goals, should development continue.
